transcript: On March 8, Venus and Saturn will cozy up for a conjunction in the evening sky. The pair will be about one degree apart, which is roughly the width of a single finger if you hold it at arm's length. A conjunction happens when two objects in the night sky appear close together, even if they're far apart in space. In reality, Venus and Saturn are nearly a billion miles apart. But to see the pair get close in the sky from our perspective, look close to the horizon in the western sky just after sunset.

transcript: Saturn and the Moon will share the sky on January 23rd as part of a conjunction. A conjunction happens when objects in the night sky look close together, even though they're actually far apart. To spot the pair, look to the west, and you'll see Saturn just below the moon, sparkling in the night sky.

transcript: January 10th, Jupiter will be at its most brilliant of the entire year. This night, Jupiter will be at what's called opposition, meaning that Earth will be directly between Jupiter and the Sun. In this alignment, Jupiter will appear bigger and brighter in the night sky than it will all year. Talk about starting off the New Year bright. To see Jupiter at its best this year, look to the east, and all evening long, you'll be able to see the planet in the constellation Gemini. It will be one of the brightest objects in the night sky. Only the moon and Venus will be brighter.

transcript: The Beehive cluster will be visible in the night sky throughout January. The Beehive cluster, more formally known as Messier 44 or M44, is made of at least 1,000 stars. It's an open star cluster, meaning it's a loosely bound group of stars. There are thousands of open star clusters like the Beehive in the Milky Way galaxy. To see the Beehive cluster, look to the Easter night sky just after sunset and before midnight throughout the month. Especially great nights to spot the cluster are around the middle of January, when the cluster isn't too high or too low in the sky to see. With dark skies, you might be able to spot the Beehive with just your eyes, but binoculars or small telescope will help.

transcript: We need to talk about comic-thread Atlas and its seemingly suspicious behavior. Is it possible that it's actually something else entirely? Thread Atlas is only the third object detected passing through our solar system from elsewhere in the galaxy. So it's understandable that we're all wondering what's going on. I went to Google Search and I asked AIMO the question on everyone's mind, if Thread Atlas isn't an alien spacecraft, how do we explain its bursts of acceleration? As far as Thread Atlas beating up goes, it's called non-gravitational acceleration. Basically, it's acting like a tiny rocket, but the fuel isn't alien-tech, it's the sun. Heating up the surface of the comet, blasting off gas and propelling it forward. Okay, but hold on, it's starting the needle through our solar system and not hitting anything. That feels planned. Turns out space is just big, like really big. The AIM breakdown explains it's a hyperbolic trajectory. Basically, missing the planets is statistically way more likely than hitting them, so don't worry, Thread Atlas is not a danger to Earth. In fact, it's at least two times further away than the distance between Earth and our Sun. Okay fine, but Thread Atlas is giving off a radio signal. How do you explain that? Rockstone broadcast frequencies, unless they're screaming, the search result calls it degassing, which is literally the sound of hydrogen ice melting and hissing into the void. It's not a radio transmission, it's a death rattle. But wait, ice means water, and water means life, right?

transcript: We're going to go through some questions to learn a little bit more about what it was like to be on the space station for so long and a little bit about what the camera didn't see while you were at work. Thanks for joining us, Johnny. Happy to be here. When you look back at your mission now, what is something that the camera didn't capture but really stayed with you? How much fun we have. One of the fun pranks we like to do when a satellite handover would happen right in the middle of a conference. I would love to flip us all upside down so the next thing, Houston C's, we're all upright and then we leave the connection and then when it comes back, we're all upside down bats. That's so cute. So you were on board during the celebration of the 25th anniversary of humans being on the space station. Were there parts of the celebration that we did not get to see? You think it to see just all hanging out of friends and comrades in the service module and talking about how it must have been 25 years ago with Expedition 1. We had a lot of good toasts without the juice and grape juice over the dinner table. Who or what did you feel most grateful for during the celebration? It was my spouse. I mean she held down the fort while it was gone. It's given so much of herself and sacrificed so much for me to go in this mission. As we head to the holidays, do you have a message for the teams that will still be working to support you in this space flight? We just can't thank you enough. Thank you for everything you do and continue to do for space. I think you nailed it. Thanks Johnny. Thank you.

transcript: On the evening of December 7th, the moon will have a friend in the night sky as the moon and Jupiter get close for a conjunction. During a conjunction, objects in the sky like planets and moons appear close together, even though they're actually far apart. For example, while the moon and Jupiter will buddy up this month from our view, in space, there are actually hundreds of millions of miles apart. To see the pair together, look up to the moon in the eastern sky on December 7th, and Jupiter will be just above and to the right.

transcript: The Geminied Meteor Shower will sparkle across the skies this December, peaking on the evenings of December 13th and 14th. This meteor shower is known for being bright and colorful, and it's made up of debris trailing the asteroid 3200 Faithon. To catch the Geminied's peak, look to the eastern sky all evening on December 13th and December 14th. The meteors will appear near the bright planet Jupiter. With the darkest possible skies, you could see up to 120 Geminied meteors per hour.

transcript: These 10 men and women are your 24 astronaut candidate class. Congratulations to our new class of astronauts. There's nothing that embodies the American dream more than the 10 of you today. You represent America, the greatest, most innovative, creative country that's ever existed on the face of the earth. We're going to need America's best and brightest because we have bold exploration plans for the future. It's going to take grit, it's going to take courage to actually meet this moment with these missions, with this vision. I couldn't be more honored to be the acting administrator and to lead this organization at this moment in time. We are going back to the moon. This time we're going to stay. And from what we learn on our mission to the moon, we're going to go to Mars. Over 8,000 people applied to get one of these 10 spots. One of these 10 could actually be one of the first Americans to put their boots on the Mars surface.
